name: rebase
context: Rewrite commit history by replaying commits onto a new base — interactive rebase, squash, and fixup
info: Rebasing moves or replays a series of commits onto a new base commit, rewriting their hashes in the process. It produces a linear history without merge commits. Interactive rebase (git rebase -i) is one of the most powerful git features — it lets you squash, reorder, edit, fixup, drop, and reword commits before sharing them. Agents use rebase to clean up messy work-in-progress history into logical, reviewable commits before pushing. The golden rule: never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch, as it rewrites history that others depend on. See [[git/merge]] for the non-destructive alternative, [[git/commit]] for creating the commits you'll rebase, and [[git/troubleshooting]] for recovering from bad rebases.
body:
# Git Rebase

Rebase replays commits from one branch onto another base, creating new commits with the same changes but different hashes. It produces clean, linear history.

## Basic Rebase

```bash
# Rebase current branch onto main
git checkout feature
git rebase main
```

```
Before:  main ── A ── B ── C
                  \
   feature ─────── D ── E

After:   main ── A ── B ── C
                            \
   feature ────────────────── D' ── E'
```

D' and E' have the same changes as D and E but are new commits with new hashes.

## Interactive Rebase

The most powerful git feature for history cleanup:

```bash
# Rebase last 3 commits interactively
git rebase -i HEAD~3

# Rebase onto main interactively
git rebase -i main
```

This opens an editor with a todo list:

```
pick abc1234 feat: add login form
pick def5678 fix: typo in login
pick ghi9012 feat: add validation
```

### Interactive Commands

| Command | Short | Effect |
|---------|-------|--------|
| `pick` | `p` | Keep commit as-is |
| `reword` | `r` | Keep commit, edit message |
| `edit` | `e` | Pause to amend the commit |
| `squash` | `s` | Meld into previous commit, combine messages |
| `fixup` | `f` | Meld into previous commit, discard this message |
| `drop` | `d` | Remove commit entirely |

### Common Patterns

**Squash WIP commits into one:**
```
pick abc1234 feat: add login form
fixup def5678 fix: typo in login
fixup ghi9012 wip: more login stuff
```

**Reorder commits:**
```
pick ghi9012 feat: add validation
pick abc1234 feat: add login form
```

**Edit a past commit:**
```
edit abc1234 feat: add login form
pick def5678 fix: typo in login
```
Git pauses at abc1234. Make changes, `git add`, then `git rebase --continue`.

## Rebase Onto

Replay a specific range of commits onto a new base:

```bash
# Transplant commits from feature that aren't on main onto release
git rebase --onto release main feature
```

Useful when you branched off the wrong base or need to move commits between branches.

## Resolving Rebase Conflicts

Conflicts during rebase are resolved one commit at a time:

```bash
# Conflict occurs during rebase
# Fix the conflict in the file
git add resolved-file.js
git rebase --continue

# Skip this commit entirely
git rebase --skip

# Abort and return to pre-rebase state
git rebase --abort
```

## Autosquash

Create fixup commits that automatically squash during interactive rebase:

```bash
# Create a fixup commit targeting a specific commit
git commit --fixup=abc1234

# Later, autosquash during rebase
git rebase -i --autosquash main
# The fixup commit is automatically placed after its target
```

## The Golden Rule

**Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch.** Rebase rewrites commit hashes. If others have based work on the original commits, their history will diverge and they'll need to force-reconcile.

Safe to rebase:
- Local commits not yet pushed
- Your own feature branch that nobody else uses

Never rebase:
- main/master
- Any branch others have checked out
- Published release branches

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Rebase feature branches onto main before merging** — keeps history linear
2. **Use fixup commits during development** — `git commit --fixup=<sha>` then `git rebase -i --autosquash`
3. **Squash WIP commits** — clean up "wip", "temp", "fix typo" commits before sharing
4. **Never rebase shared branches** — use [[git/merge]] instead
5. **Use --abort if confused** — you can always start over

sections.title: Basic rebase
sections.body: Run `git rebase <base>` to replay current branch commits onto `<base>`. This creates new commits with new hashes but identical changes. The branch pointer moves to the new tip. Common pattern: `git checkout feature && git rebase main` to update a feature branch with the latest main changes, producing linear history.

sections.title: Interactive rebase
sections.body: Run `git rebase -i <base>` to open an editor with the commit todo list. Commands: pick (keep), reword (edit message), edit (pause to modify), squash (meld with previous, combine messages), fixup (meld with previous, discard message), drop (remove). Commits execute top-to-bottom. Reorder lines to reorder commits. Delete lines to drop commits. This is the primary tool for cleaning up history before sharing.

sections.title: Rebase --onto for transplanting
sections.body: Use `git rebase --onto <newbase> <upstream> <branch>` to transplant a range of commits. This replays commits reachable from `<branch>` but not from `<upstream>` onto `<newbase>`. Use case: moving commits from one base branch to another, or extracting a subset of commits from a longer branch.

sections.title: Autosquash workflow
sections.body: Create fixup commits with `git commit --fixup=<target-sha>`. When you later run `git rebase -i --autosquash <base>`, fixup commits are automatically reordered to follow their targets and marked as fixup. This is the cleanest workflow for iterative development: make changes, create fixup commits, then autosquash before pushing.

sections.title: Handling rebase conflicts
sections.body: During rebase, conflicts are resolved one commit at a time. Fix the conflicted file, `git add` it, then `git rebase --continue` to proceed to the next commit. Use `git rebase --skip` to drop a commit that's entirely conflicted. Use `git rebase --abort` to cancel and return to the original state. Unlike merge conflicts (resolved once), rebase conflicts may recur for each replayed commit.

sections.title: The golden rule of rebase
sections.body: Never rebase commits that exist on a remote branch that others use. Rebase rewrites history — every rebased commit gets a new SHA. If collaborators have based work on the original SHAs, they'll need to reconcile divergent history. Safe: local branches, personal feature branches. Unsafe: main, develop, release branches, any shared branch. When in doubt, use [[git/merge]] instead.

links.context: Recover from bad rebases with reflog
Force-push after rebasing a pushed branch

related.note: Merge preserves history; rebase rewrites it for linearity
Reflog can recover commits lost during a bad rebase

scripts.name: Interactive Rebase Helper
scripts.description: Start an interactive rebase with autosquash support
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

BASE="${1:?Usage: interactive-rebase.sh <base> [--autosquash]}"
AUTOSQUASH=""

if [ "${2:-}" = "--autosquash" ]; then
  AUTOSQUASH="--autosquash"
fi

# Show what will be rebased
COMMIT_COUNT=$(git rev-list --count "$BASE"..HEAD)
echo "Rebasing $COMMIT_COUNT commits onto $BASE"
echo
git log --oneline "$BASE"..HEAD
echo

if [ "$COMMIT_COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "Nothing to rebase — HEAD is already on or behind $BASE"
  exit 0
fi

# Check for fixup commits
FIXUPS=$(git log --oneline "$BASE"..HEAD | grep -c '^[a-f0-9]* fixup!' || true)
if [ "$FIXUPS" -gt 0 ] && [ -z "$AUTOSQUASH" ]; then
  echo "Found $FIXUPS fixup commit(s). Consider using --autosquash."
fi

git rebase -i $AUTOSQUASH "$BASE"

newHead.description: SHA of new HEAD after rebase

depends.reason: Must be on a branch to rebase

author: dojo-community
